source: Centenarian Coach Recovery Notes - Sleep Duration and Longevity
content: Most adults need 7 to 9 hours of sleep per night for full physical and cognitive recovery. Chronically sleeping under 7 hours is associated with higher cardiovascular risk, impaired glucose control, and weaker immune function. For people training regularly, the upper half of that range supports tissue repair and hormone balance, and protects the strength and muscle gains made in the gym.

source: Centenarian Coach Recovery Notes - Deep and REM Sleep
content: Sleep is not uniform. Deep (slow-wave) sleep dominates the first half of the night and drives physical repair, growth-hormone release, and immune work. REM sleep concentrates in the second half and supports memory consolidation and emotional regulation. Cutting sleep short trims REM disproportionately, so a full night matters beyond just total hours.

source: Centenarian Coach Recovery Notes - HRV as a Recovery Signal
content: Heart-rate variability (HRV) is the variation in time between heartbeats and reflects autonomic nervous system balance. A higher HRV relative to your own baseline generally signals good recovery and readiness to train hard; a HRV trending below baseline signals accumulated stress, under-recovery, or illness. HRV is highly individual, so trends against your personal baseline matter far more than comparisons to other people.

source: Centenarian Coach Recovery Notes - Rest Days and Supercompensation
content: Training applies stress; adaptation happens during rest. Supercompensation is the rebound where, given enough recovery, the body rebuilds slightly stronger than before. Training the same hard pattern again before that rebound completes blunts progress. Most people benefit from at least one or two lower-stress or full rest days per week, scheduled deliberately rather than taken only when exhausted.

source: Centenarian Coach Recovery Notes - Systemic vs Local Recovery
content: Recovery is both local (a trained muscle) and systemic (the nervous, hormonal, and immune systems as a whole). A muscle group may feel fresh in 48 hours while systemic fatigue from heavy or frequent sessions lingers longer. Poor sleep, life stress, and under-eating all tax systemic recovery, which is why training quality can fall even when no single muscle feels sore.

source: Centenarian Coach Recovery Notes - Sleep Hygiene
content: Sleep quality responds to consistent habits. A regular sleep and wake time stabilizes the circadian clock. A cool, dark, quiet room supports deeper sleep. Limiting caffeine after early afternoon, reducing bright and blue light in the last hour before bed, and avoiding heavy meals and alcohol late all measurably improve sleep efficiency, the share of time in bed actually spent asleep.

A short nap of 10 to 20 minutes can restore alertness without grogginess and does not usually disturb night sleep when taken early in the afternoon. Longer naps risk waking from deep sleep and can push back the night's sleep onset. Naps supplement but do not replace adequate night sleep; relying on long daytime naps often signals an insufficient or low-quality night.

source: Centenarian Coach Recovery Notes - Circadian Rhythm and Light
content: The circadian clock is set largely by light. Getting bright outdoor light within an hour of waking advances and strengthens the rhythm, improving daytime alertness and night-time sleep pressure. Dimming indoor light in the evening does the reverse signal. A stable light-dark pattern is one of the most reliable, low-effort levers for better recovery.

source: Centenarian Coach Recovery Notes - Signs of Under-Recovery
content: Under-recovery and overreaching show up as a cluster of signs: stalled or declining performance, elevated resting heart rate, HRV trending below baseline, disrupted sleep, low motivation, irritability, and nagging minor injuries. The response is not to push harder; it is to reduce training stress, prioritize sleep and nutrition, and let systemic recovery catch up before resuming hard work.
